enrichment.short_description: OhMD is a HIPAA-compliant patient communication platform that lets medical practices text, call, and message patients from their existing landline, with an AI assistant to automate routine calls.
enrichment.seo_title: OhMD Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OhMD review 2026: HIPAA-compliant patient texting and AI call automation for medical practices. See pricing, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OhMD is a HIPAA-compliant patient communication and engagement platform built for medical, dental, and specialty healthcare practices. It was co-founded in 2015 by CEO Ethan Bechtel in Burlington, Vermont, after his own experience with repeated orthopedic injuries exposed how inefficient phone-based patient communication was. OhMD's core product lets practices text-enable their existing office landline so patients can send and receive secure two-way SMS and RCS messages without either party downloading an app. The platform bundles voice and video calling, voicemail transcription, call-to-text conversion, a website chat widget, internal team messaging, automated workflows, and broadcast messaging into a single inbox, all covered by a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) and multi-factor authentication to satisfy HIPAA requirements. Its key differentiator versus generic business texting tools (like Podium or Weave) is a purpose-built healthcare focus: EHR integrations, patient-specific automated workflows (appointment reminders, intake, recalls), and an AI voice and text assistant called Nia that answers routine phone calls, books openings on the calendar, and routes complex calls to staff, directly targeting the high call volume that burdens front-desk teams. OhMD sells three tiers: Communicate ($300/month) for practices that primarily need HIPAA-compliant texting and calling; Automate ($500/month) which adds the Nia AI assistant, automatic message routing, and EHR integration, with calling usage billed separately; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier for multi-location health systems needing SSO, dedicated support, and API access. OhMD is a small, privately held company (roughly 40-50 employees) that has historically positioned itself as a free-to-use texting layer for eliminating 'phone tag,' later evolving into a paid, AI-augmented communication suite. Target customers are small to mid-sized outpatient practices, dental offices, and physical therapy or specialty clinics that get most patient contact by phone and want to cut hold times and no-shows. Primary competitors and alternatives include Klara, Weave, Podium, Solutionreach, and Luma Health, most of which compete on similar two-way texting plus AI receptionist positioning for healthcare and dental verticals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OhMD is a patient communication platform designed specifically for healthcare practices that need to text, call, and message patients while staying HIPAA compliant. Founded in 2015 by Ethan Bechtel in Burlington, Vermont, the company built its reputation on a simple idea: let patients text the same landline number they already call, without requiring either side to install an app.

Today OhMD has grown beyond basic texting into a fuller communication suite that includes voice and video calling, an AI phone assistant, website chat, and EHR-connected automated workflows, aimed at reducing the call volume that overwhelms front-desk staff at small and mid-sized practices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OhMD's core is two-way SMS and RCS texting tied to a practice's existing landline, paired with voice and video calling, voicemail transcription, and call-to-text conversion so incoming calls can be redirected into a text conversation.

On top of messaging, OhMD adds a website chat widget, internal team messaging, automated patient workflows (reminders, intake forms, recalls), and broadcast messaging for practice-wide announcements. The Automate plan layers in Nia, an AI voice and text assistant that can answer routine calls, handle scheduling questions, and route complex calls to staff, along with EHR integrations for syncing patient records.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OhMD publishes two named plans on its pricing page: Communicate starting at $300/month for two-way texting, calling, and team messaging, and Automate starting at $500/month for everything in Communicate plus the Nia AI assistant, automatic call/message routing, and EHR integration (calling usage is billed separately).

Larger multi-location organizations can move to a custom-priced Enterprise plan that adds SSO/identity management, dedicated support, multi-tenant management, reporting, and API access, typically with an annual billing commitment and volume discounts.

features.title: Landline texting
features.description: Enables secure two-way SMS and RCS texting on a practice's existing office landline number, so no new number or app is required for patients.

features.title: Voice and video calling
features.description: Built-in voice and video calling lets staff handle appointments, follow-ups, and telehealth visits from the same inbox as text messages.

features.title: Nia AI assistant
features.description: An AI voice and text assistant (available on the Automate plan) answers routine patient calls, handles common questions, and routes complex issues to staff.

features.title: Automated workflows
features.description: Configurable workflows send appointment reminders, intake forms, and recall messages automatically based on practice-defined triggers.

features.title: Website chat widget
features.description: A chat widget can be embedded on a practice website so visitors can text the practice directly from a browser.

features.title: Call-to-text and voicemail transcription
features.description: Incoming calls can be converted into text conversations, and voicemails are automatically transcribed for faster staff review.

features.title: EHR integration
features.description: Automate and Enterprise plans connect to electronic health record systems to sync patient data and reduce duplicate entry.

features.title: HIPAA compliance and BAA
features.description: All plans include a signed Business Associate Agreement and multi-factor authentication to meet HIPAA security requirements for patient communication.

pros: Patients can text a practice's existing landline number without installing an app
HIPAA compliance and a signed BAA are included on every plan
Nia AI assistant can absorb routine call volume for busy front desks
Combines texting, calling, chat, and team messaging in one inbox
Purpose-built for healthcare workflows like intake, reminders, and recalls
cons: Pricing starts at $300/month, higher than many general-purpose business texting tools
Automate plan's calling usage is billed separately on top of the base subscription
AI assistant (Nia) and EHR integration require the more expensive Automate tier
Enterprise pricing is custom and not published, requiring a sales conversation
Smaller company size (roughly 40-50 employees) than larger healthcare-communication rivals

faqs.question: What is OhMD used for?
faqs.answer: OhMD is a HIPAA-compliant patient communication platform that lets healthcare practices text, call, and message patients from their existing office landline, reducing phone tag and call volume.

faqs.question: Is OhMD HIPAA compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes. OhMD includes a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) and multi-factor authentication on every plan to meet HIPAA requirements for handling patient information.

faqs.question: How much does OhMD cost?
faqs.answer: OhMD's published plans start at $300/month for the Communicate plan and $500/month for the Automate plan, which adds the Nia AI assistant and EHR integration. Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Do patients need to download an app to use OhMD?
faqs.answer: No. OhMD lets patients text a practice's existing landline number using standard SMS or RCS messaging, so no app download is required on either side.

faqs.question: What is Nia in OhMD?
faqs.answer: Nia is OhMD's AI voice and text assistant, available on the Automate plan, which can answer routine patient calls, handle common scheduling questions, and route more complex calls to staff.

faqs.question: Does OhMD integrate with EHR systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, EHR integration is available on the Automate and Enterprise plans to sync patient records and reduce duplicate data entry for front-desk staff.

faqs.question: Who founded OhMD?
faqs.answer: OhMD was co-founded in 2015 by CEO Ethan Bechtel and is headquartered in Burlington, Vermont.

use_cases.title: Reducing front-desk call volume
use_cases.description: Practices deflect routine phone calls into text conversations and AI-handled calls, freeing front-desk staff to focus on in-person patients.
use_cases.audience: Small and mid-sized medical or dental practices

use_cases.title: Appointment reminders and recalls
use_cases.description: Automated workflows text patients reminders, recall notices, and intake forms ahead of visits to reduce no-shows.

use_cases.title: Telehealth and follow-up care
use_cases.description: Voice and video calling combined with texting lets providers run telehealth visits and post-visit follow-ups from a single tool.
use_cases.audience: Physicians, physical therapists, and specialty clinics

features: Nia AI voice and text assistant

enrichment.short_description: Okta is a publicly traded identity and access management platform providing single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and lifecycle management for workforce and customer identity.
enrichment.seo_title: Okta Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Okta review 2026: identity and access management with SSO, MFA, and lifecycle management. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Okta, Inc. is a publicly traded (NASDAQ: OKTA) identity and access management (IAM) company founded in 2009 by former Salesforce executives Todd McKinnon and Frederic Kerrest, headquartered in San Francisco, California. Okta was an early cloud-native alternative to on-premise directory and SSO tools like Active Directory Federation Services, and it went public in an IPO in 2017. In 2021 Okta acquired competitor Auth0 for roughly $6.5 billion, giving it two distinct product lines: Okta Workforce Identity Cloud for employee-facing SSO, MFA, and lifecycle management, and Okta Customer Identity Cloud (built on the former Auth0 platform) for developers embedding login, authentication, and authorization into customer-facing applications. Core capabilities span single sign-on (SSO) across thousands of pre-built app integrations, adaptive multi-factor authentication (MFA), Universal Directory for centralizing user profiles, Lifecycle Management for automating provisioning and deprovisioning, Privileged Access, Identity Governance, Identity Threat Protection, and API Access Management. Okta serves over 19,000 customers ranging from mid-market businesses to large enterprises and government agencies, and employs roughly 6,000 people worldwide. Workforce Identity pricing is tiered and per-user, billed annually: Starter at $6/user/month covers SSO, MFA, Universal Directory, and limited workflow automation; Core Essentials at $14/user/month and Essentials at $17/user/month add adaptive MFA, privileged access, lifecycle management, and access governance; Professional and Enterprise tiers are custom-priced and add device access, identity security posture management, identity threat protection, API access management, and access gateway capabilities. A minimum annual contract value (commonly cited around $1,500) applies to workforce plans. Customer Identity Cloud pricing starts around $3,000/month for an Enterprise Base Platform with unlimited OIDC/SAML apps, with B2C and B2B suite add-ons priced individually. Okta's key differentiators are its breadth of pre-integrated applications, neutrality as a standalone identity vendor (versus identity features bundled into Microsoft Entra ID or Google Workspace), and strong developer tooling inherited from Auth0. Primary competitors include Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD), Ping Identity, OneLogin, JumpCloud, ForgeRock, and Google Cloud Identity. Okta targets IT and security teams at mid-market and enterprise organizations that need centralized, vendor-neutral identity and access control across both internal employee systems and external customer-facing applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Okta was founded in 2009 by Todd McKinnon and Frederic Kerrest, two former Salesforce executives who saw an opportunity to build cloud-native identity management as enterprises moved away from on-premise directories. Headquartered in San Francisco, the company went public in 2017 and significantly expanded its footprint in 2021 by acquiring Auth0, a leading developer-focused identity platform, for approximately $6.5 billion.

The combined company now operates two product suites: Okta Workforce Identity Cloud, which secures employee access to internal systems, and Okta Customer Identity Cloud (built on Auth0's technology), which lets developers add login, authentication, and authorization to customer-facing apps. Okta serves more than 19,000 customers globally and employs around 6,000 people.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Okta's Workforce Identity Cloud centers on single sign-on across thousands of pre-built application integrations, adaptive multi-factor authentication, and a Universal Directory that centralizes user identities across systems. Lifecycle Management automates provisioning and deprovisioning as employees join, change roles, or leave.

Higher tiers add Privileged Access management, Identity Governance and access certification, Identity Threat Protection, Device Access controls, and API Access Management. Okta Customer Identity Cloud (Auth0) provides developer SDKs, prebuilt login flows, and fine-grained authorization for consumer and B2B applications, including inbound federation for enterprise customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Workforce Identity is sold per user per month, billed annually, starting with Starter at $6/user/month for core SSO and MFA, moving through Core Essentials ($14/user/month) and Essentials ($17/user/month) for adaptive MFA, lifecycle management, and access governance, up to custom-priced Professional and Enterprise tiers with the fullest feature set. A minimum annual contract applies.

Customer Identity Cloud pricing is structured differently, starting around $3,000/month for an Enterprise Base Platform with unlimited OIDC and SAML applications, with B2C Suite and B2B Suite add-ons priced individually based on monthly active users and required capabilities.

features.title: Single sign-on (SSO)
features.description: Provides one-click access across thousands of pre-integrated cloud and on-premise applications through Okta's app catalog.

features.title: Adaptive multi-factor authentication
features.description: Context-aware MFA adjusts authentication requirements based on device, location, and risk signals.

features.title: Universal Directory
features.description: Centralizes user, group, and device profiles from multiple sources into a single identity directory.

features.title: Lifecycle Management
features.description: Automates user provisioning and deprovisioning across connected applications as employment status changes.

features.title: Identity Governance
features.description: Supports access certification, entitlement management, and compliance reporting for regulated industries.

features.title: Privileged Access
features.description: Secures and audits access to sensitive systems and privileged accounts.

features.title: Customer Identity Cloud (Auth0)
features.description: Developer-focused authentication and authorization APIs for embedding login into customer-facing applications.

features.title: API Access Management
features.description: Secures machine-to-machine and API traffic with OAuth 2.0 token issuance and scoped access controls.

pros: Very large catalog of pre-built application integrations for fast SSO rollout
Vendor-neutral identity platform that works across Microsoft, Google, AWS, and other ecosystems
Strong developer tooling for customer identity inherited from the Auth0 acquisition
Mature governance, lifecycle, and privileged access features for regulated enterprises
Established public company with long operating history and enterprise-grade reliability
cons: Per-user pricing can become expensive at scale for large workforces
Advanced features like governance and threat protection require higher, custom-priced tiers
Minimum annual contract value can be a barrier for very small organizations
Two separate product lines (Workforce and Customer Identity) can add complexity when evaluating pricing
Has been the target of high-profile security incidents in past years, drawing scrutiny of its own security practices

faqs.question: What does Okta do?
faqs.answer: Okta is an identity and access management platform that provides single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, directory services, and lifecycle management for both employee (workforce) and customer-facing applications.

faqs.question: How much does Okta cost?
faqs.answer: Okta Workforce Identity plans start at $6/user/month for the Starter tier, with Core Essentials and Essentials tiers at $14 and $17/user/month respectively; Professional and Enterprise tiers, along with Customer Identity Cloud plans, are custom-priced.

faqs.question: Is Okta the same as Auth0?
faqs.answer: No, but they are related. Okta acquired Auth0 in 2021. Auth0 now operates as Okta Customer Identity Cloud, a separate product line focused on developer-facing customer authentication, alongside Okta's original Workforce Identity Cloud.

faqs.question: When was Okta founded?
faqs.answer: Okta was founded in 2009 by Todd McKinnon and Frederic Kerrest and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. It went public in 2017.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Okta?
faqs.answer: Common Okta alternatives include Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD), Ping Identity, OneLogin, JumpCloud, ForgeRock, and Google Cloud Identity.

faqs.question: Does Okta support multi-factor authentication?
faqs.answer: Yes, Okta provides adaptive MFA that adjusts authentication challenges based on contextual risk signals like device, location, and network.

faqs.question: Is there a minimum contract for Okta?
faqs.answer: Yes, Okta's Workforce Identity plans typically require a minimum annual contract value, commonly cited around $1,500, in addition to per-user monthly pricing.

use_cases.title: Enterprise workforce single sign-on

use_cases.audience: Compliance, security, and IT operations teams

Foundational Customer Identity Cloud (Auth0) platform access

enrichment.short_description: Olark is a live chat and AI website agent platform that answers visitor questions, qualifies leads, and routes conversations to human agents for sales and support teams.
enrichment.seo_title: Olark Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Olark review 2026: AI website agent and live chat for sales and support. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and top Olark alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Olark is a live chat and AI website agent company founded in 2009 as part of the Y Combinator accelerator program, originally launched by four co-founders working out of a house in Palo Alto, California. It has since grown into a fully remote, privately held company serving more than 11,000 paying businesses worldwide, with a small team estimated between roughly 20 and 35 employees. Olark's original product was human-staffed live chat for websites; it has since layered an AI website agent on top, which automatically answers unlimited visitor questions using a business's existing knowledge base and FAQs, captures and qualifies leads, proactively engages visitors based on behavior, and hands off conversations to a human agent when needed. Olark differentiates itself from AI-first competitors like Intercom and Drift with a flat-fee pricing philosophy rather than token-based or per-resolution billing: its Standard AI Website Agent plan costs $400/month billed annually ($4,800/year), which includes the AI agent, one human agent seat, and unlimited AI-handled conversations with no token limits or usage overages regardless of traffic spikes. Additional human agent seats cost $29/agent/month. For teams that only want traditional human-staffed live chat without AI, Olark also offers a Live Chat Only plan at $29/agent/month, a continuation of its original 2009-era chat product. Custom Enterprise plans are available for organizations needing regional routing, multiple teams, advanced integrations, custom workflows, SLAs, legal reviews, and single sign-on. All plans include a free trial with no credit card required. Olark integrates with common CRM, help desk, and e-commerce platforms (such as Salesforce, HubSpot, Shopify, and Zendesk) and is positioned for small and mid-sized businesses in sales, marketing, and customer support that want an affordable, predictable-cost alternative to enterprise conversational AI platforms. Its main competitors include Intercom, Drift, LiveChat, Tidio, and Zendesk Chat (Zendesk Messaging).

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Olark was founded in 2009 through Y Combinator by four co-founders operating out of a Palo Alto house, initially as a straightforward live chat widget for websites. Over more than fifteen years the company has grown into a fully remote team serving over 11,000 paying businesses, while staying deliberately small compared to venture-backed rivals like Intercom and Drift.

The product has evolved from pure human-staffed chat into an AI website agent that answers visitor questions around the clock, with the option to fall back to human agents for more complex conversations, all managed from Olark's chat dashboard and mobile apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Olark's AI Website Agent answers unlimited visitor questions by drawing on a business's existing FAQs and knowledge base, automatically keeping its answers up to date as content changes. It captures and qualifies leads, proactively engages visitors based on browsing behavior, and routes conversations to human agents when needed.

Beyond the AI layer, Olark provides real-time visitor insights, chat transcripts and reporting, targeted proactive chat triggers, and integrations with CRM, help desk, and e-commerce tools. Enterprise customers can add regional routing across multiple teams, custom workflows, SLAs, and single sign-on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Olark's primary plan, the Standard AI Website Agent, costs $400/month when billed annually ($4,800/year) and includes the AI agent plus one human agent seat with no token limits or usage-based overages; additional human seats cost $29/agent/month.

Businesses that only need traditional human live chat, without the AI layer, can choose the Live Chat Only plan at $29/agent/month. Larger organizations with complex routing, integration, or compliance needs can request a custom Enterprise plan. A free trial is available with no credit card required.

features.title: CRM and help desk integrations
features.description: Connects with tools like Salesforce, HubSpot, Shopify, and Zendesk to sync chat data with existing workflows.

features.title: Flat-fee pricing with no overages

Long operating history since 2009 with a large base of over 11,000 paying customers

faqs.question: What is Olark used for?
faqs.answer: Olark is a live chat and AI website agent platform that lets businesses answer visitor questions, capture and qualify leads, and route conversations between AI and human agents on their website.

faqs.question: How much does Olark cost?
faqs.answer: Olark's Standard AI Website Agent plan costs $400/month billed annually and includes one human agent seat, with extra seats at $29/agent/month. A Live Chat Only plan without AI is available for $29/agent/month, and custom Enterprise pricing is available.

faqs.question: Does Olark use token-based pricing for its AI agent?
faqs.answer: No. Olark uses a flat-fee pricing model with no token limits or usage overages, so a business's bill does not spike during high-traffic periods.

faqs.question: When was Olark founded?
faqs.answer: Olark was founded in 2009 as part of the Y Combinator accelerator by four co-founders working out of a house in Palo Alto, California.

faqs.question: Can Olark route chats to a human agent?
faqs.answer: Yes, the AI Website Agent can hand off conversations to a human agent when a visitor's question needs personal attention.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Olark?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Intercom, Drift, LiveChat, Tidio, and Zendesk Chat.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Olark?
faqs.answer: Yes, Olark offers a free trial with no credit card required before committing to a paid plan.

enrichment.short_description: Olive is a free, open-source, cross-platform non-linear video editor with node-based compositing, aiming to give hobbyists and indie creators a free alternative to professional editing suites.
enrichment.seo_title: Olive Video Editor Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Olive Video Editor review 2026: free open-source non-linear video editor with node-based compositing. Features, status, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Olive Video Editor is a free, open-source, non-linear video editing application for Linux, Windows, and macOS, released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3). Development began in late 2017, led by primary maintainer MattKC, with the first public pre-release build (version 0.1.0) shipping on May 2, 2019. It is maintained by the volunteer-run Olive Team, a small collective of roughly 10-15 contributors coordinating through GitHub rather than a commercial company, so it has no headquarters, employee count, or funding in the traditional sense. Olive is written in C++ using the Qt framework for its interface, FFmpeg for encoding and decoding, OpenImageIO and OpenColorIO for image handling and color management, and CMake for its build system. Its headline differentiator versus other free editors like Shotcut, OpenShot, and Kdenlive is node-based compositing: rather than a fixed effects stack, users build up effects and transitions as a graph of connected nodes, offering the kind of modular, non-destructive workflow more commonly found in paid tools like Nuke or DaVinci Resolve's Fusion page. Olive also includes a caching system that pre-renders portions of the timeline to disk as small images to keep scrubbing and previewing responsive on modest hardware. Because Olive is entirely free and open source, there is no pricing tier, subscription, or paid version; the project is sustained through volunteer contributions rather than commercial revenue. Olive remains labeled as alpha software by its own developers and documentation, and has been described by the project itself as 'highly unstable,' reflecting a large-scale rewrite the codebase underwent after early architectural problems made the original version unsustainable to maintain. This makes Olive best suited to hobbyists, students, and open-source enthusiasts experimenting with node-based editing concepts rather than professionals needing production-stable software for paid client work. Competing free and open-source alternatives include Shotcut, Kdenlive, OpenShot, and Blender's built-in Video Sequence Editor, while professional alternatives with more mature stability include DaVinci Resolve (free and paid tiers) and Adobe Premiere Pro.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Olive Video Editor is a free, open-source, cross-platform non-linear video editor for Linux, Windows, and macOS. Development began in late 2017 under primary maintainer MattKC, with the first public pre-release, version 0.1.0, shipping in May 2019. The project is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 and maintained by the volunteer Olive Team rather than a commercial company.

Olive was created to give creators a free alternative to high-end professional editing software, with a particular focus on node-based effects compositing that was largely missing from other open-source editors at the time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Olive's defining feature is node-based compositing, which lets users build effects and transitions as a graph of connected nodes instead of a fixed linear effects stack, offering more flexibility and reducing reliance on third-party plugins.

The editor also includes a caching system that pre-renders part or all of a sequence to disk as small preview images, speeding up scrubbing and playback, along with standard non-linear editing tools built on FFmpeg for broad format support and OpenColorIO for color management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Olive Video Editor is completely free and open source under the GPLv3 license. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or premium feature unlocks; the full application and source code are available at no cost from the project's GitHub repository and website.

Because Olive is community-maintained, users can also build from source or contribute code, translations, and bug reports rather than paying for support, though this also means there is no commercial support contract available.

features.title: Node-based compositing
features.description: Effects and transitions are built as a graph of connected nodes rather than a fixed stack, offering flexible, modular compositing similar to professional tools.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs natively on Linux, Windows, and macOS from the same open-source codebase.

features.title: Disk caching for smooth playback
features.description: Pre-renders portions of the timeline to disk as small images to keep scrubbing and preview playback responsive.

features.title: FFmpeg-based format support
features.description: Uses FFmpeg for encoding and decoding, giving broad compatibility with common video and audio formats.

features.title: OpenColorIO color management
features.description: Integrates OpenColorIO for consistent, professional-grade color management across a project.

features.title: Free and open source
features.description: Released under GPLv3 with publicly available source code, free to use, modify, and redistribute.

pros: Completely free with no paid tiers or subscriptions
Node-based compositing offers more flexible effects workflows than many free editors
Cross-platform on Linux, Windows, and macOS
Open-source codebase can be inspected, modified, and contributed to by anyone
Active GitHub community continues to release nightly and pre-release builds
cons: Officially labeled alpha software and described by developers as highly unstable
No commercial support; help comes from community forums and GitHub issues only
Smaller plugin and template ecosystem compared to commercial editors
Steeper learning curve for users unfamiliar with node-based compositing
Volunteer-driven development pace can be slower and less predictable than funded competitors

faqs.question: Is Olive Video Editor free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Olive Video Editor is completely free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 3, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: What platforms does Olive Video Editor support?
faqs.answer: Olive runs on Linux, Windows, and macOS from a single open-source codebase.

faqs.question: Is Olive Video Editor stable enough for professional work?
faqs.answer: Olive is currently labeled alpha software by its own developers and is described as highly unstable, so it is generally better suited to hobbyist and experimental projects than mission-critical professional work.

faqs.question: What makes Olive different from other free video editors?
faqs.answer: Olive's key differentiator is node-based compositing, which lets users build effects and transitions as a graph of connected nodes for more flexible, modular editing than a fixed effects stack.

faqs.question: Who develops Olive Video Editor?
faqs.answer: Olive is developed by the volunteer-run Olive Team, a community of roughly 10-15 contributors, led originally by developer MattKC, who began the project in late 2017.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Olive Video Editor?
faqs.answer: Popular alternatives include Shotcut, Kdenlive, OpenShot, Blender's Video Sequence Editor, and DaVinci Resolve.

faqs.question: Does Olive Video Editor support 4K and other high-resolution footage?
faqs.answer: Olive uses FFmpeg for decoding and encoding, giving it broad format and resolution support, though performance depends on the caching settings and available hardware.

use_cases.title: Hobbyist and student video editing
use_cases.description: Individuals learning video editing use Olive as a free, no-commitment alternative to paid software.

use_cases.title: Experimenting with node-based compositing
use_cases.description: Editors curious about node-based effects workflows use Olive to learn concepts also found in tools like Nuke or Fusion.

use_cases.title: Open-source contribution and development
use_cases.description: Developers contribute code, bug fixes, or translations to the Olive project on GitHub.

features: Community support via GitHub and forums

enrichment.short_description: OliveTin is a free, self-hosted web interface, written in Go, that gives safe and simple access to predefined shell commands, popular with homelab and self-hosting users.
enrichment.seo_title: OliveTin Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OliveTin review 2026: self-hosted web UI for running predefined shell commands safely. Features, setup, pros, cons, and alternatives for homelab users.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OliveTin is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application that gives safe and simple access to predefined shell commands from a web interface, without exposing a full terminal or SSH access. It was created by developer James Read, who introduced the project publicly in mid-2021, making it roughly five years old as of 2026. OliveTin is written in Go for its backend, uses gRPC/Connect-RPC for its API, and ships a modern single-page-app web frontend that is responsive and touch-friendly, making it usable from phones and tablets as well as desktops. The project is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0) and is entirely free, with no paid tier, company, or commercial entity behind it; it is maintained as an independent open-source project on GitHub under the OliveTin organization. Its core use case is letting a homelab operator or sysadmin define a fixed list of shell commands or scripts in a simple YAML configuration file (for example, restarting a Docker container, rebooting a Raspberry Pi, or triggering a backup script), which then appear as clickable buttons on a lightweight web dashboard. This lets non-technical household members, or less-trusted users, safely trigger specific pre-approved actions without ever having shell or SSH access to the underlying server. OliveTin explicitly avoids telemetry, analytics, tracking, and ads, and does not require any internet connection to function, positioning it firmly as a privacy-respecting, self-hosted tool for the home server and homelab community. It uses only a few megabytes of RAM and minimal CPU, so it can run happily on constrained hardware like a Raspberry Pi alongside other self-hosted services such as Ombi, Sonarr, or Home Assistant. Because access control is central to its safety model, OliveTin has also been the subject of security research; a 2026 CVE (CVE-2026-27626) documented OS command injection vectors in its Shell-mode execution path, underscoring the importance of running it behind proper authentication and keeping it updated. There is no commercial competitor in the traditional sense; the closest alternatives are general-purpose tools like Cockpit, Webmin, Ansible Semaphore, or custom cron/webhook scripts that homelab users might otherwise cobble together.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OliveTin is a free, open-source, self-hosted web interface that exposes a curated list of predefined shell commands as clickable buttons, rather than giving users full shell or SSH access. It was created by developer James Read and first introduced publicly in mid-2021, and has since become a popular homelab and self-hosting tool.

The project is written in Go, uses a gRPC/Connect-RPC API, and ships a responsive single-page-app frontend, all released under the AGPL-3.0 open-source license with no company or commercial offering behind it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Administrators define available actions in a simple YAML configuration file, mapping each action to a shell command or script; OliveTin then renders these as buttons on a web dashboard that any authorized user can click to trigger the underlying command.

The interface is touch-friendly and works well on phones and tablets, uses only a few megabytes of RAM, includes no telemetry, tracking, or ads, and requires no internet connection to operate, making it well suited to constrained, self-hosted hardware like a Raspberry Pi.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OliveTin is completely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license. There is no paid version, subscription, or commercial tier; users self-host it on their own hardware, typically via a .deb package or Docker container.

Because there is no company behind the project, support is provided through the open-source community, GitHub issues, and the developer's own blog and social channels rather than a paid support contract.

features.title: Predefined shell command execution
features.description: Exposes a curated list of shell commands or scripts as clickable actions, avoiding the need to grant full shell access.

features.title: YAML-based configuration
features.description: Actions are configured through a simple, human-readable YAML file, making setup and version control straightforward.

features.title: Responsive, touch-friendly web UI
features.description: The single-page-app interface works well on desktops, tablets, and phones for triggering actions from any device.

features.title: Lightweight footprint
features.description: Uses only a few megabytes of RAM and minimal CPU, suitable for constrained hardware like a Raspberry Pi.

features.title: No telemetry or internet dependency
features.description: Includes no usage tracking, analytics, or ads, and does not require an internet connection to function.

features.title: REST and Connect-RPC API
features.description: Exposes a documented API so other tools or dashboards can trigger the same predefined actions programmatically.

pros: Completely free and open source with an active independent developer
Very lightweight, runs well on low-powered hardware like a Raspberry Pi
No telemetry, tracking, ads, or required internet connection
Simple YAML configuration makes setup approachable for homelab users
Touch-friendly web UI works well for non-technical household members
cons: No commercial support; help relies on community and GitHub issues
Security researchers have documented OS command injection vulnerabilities (e.g. CVE-2026-27626), requiring careful configuration and updates
Limited to executing predefined commands, not a general-purpose remote management tool
Maintained by a single primary developer, which is a bus-factor risk for long-term continuity
Requires basic comfort with YAML and self-hosting to configure and deploy

faqs.question: What is OliveTin used for?
faqs.answer: OliveTin is a self-hosted web interface that lets users safely trigger a predefined list of shell commands or scripts from a web dashboard, without granting full shell or SSH access.

faqs.question: Is OliveTin free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OliveTin is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with no paid tier or subscription.

faqs.question: Who created OliveTin?
faqs.answer: OliveTin was created by developer James Read, who introduced the project publicly in mid-2021.

faqs.question: What programming language is OliveTin written in?
faqs.answer: OliveTin's backend is written in Go, with a web-based single-page-app frontend using a REST and Connect-RPC API.

faqs.question: Is OliveTin secure?
faqs.answer: OliveTin is designed to limit users to predefined commands, but like any tool that executes shell commands, it should be run behind authentication and kept updated; a 2026 CVE documented command injection issues in its shell execution mode.

faqs.question: Can OliveTin run on a Raspberry Pi?
faqs.answer: Yes, OliveTin uses only a few megabytes of RAM and minimal CPU, making it well suited to low-powered devices like a Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: Does OliveTin require an internet connection?
faqs.answer: No, OliveTin does not require an internet connection for any of its functionality and includes no telemetry or tracking.

use_cases.title: Constrained-hardware automation

enrichment.short_description: Ollama is an open-source platform for downloading, managing, and running large language models locally on your own hardware, with an optional paid cloud tier for offloading heavier workloads.
enrichment.seo_title: Ollama Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ollama review 2026: run open-source LLMs locally for free, or scale with Ollama Cloud. Compare features, pricing plans, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ollama is an open-source platform for downloading, managing, and running large language models (LLMs) locally on a user's own hardware, rather than through a cloud API. The company was founded in 2023 by Jeffrey Morgan and Michael Chiang, both alumni of Y Combinator's Winter 2021 batch, and is based in Palo Alto, California. As of mid-2026 Ollama has raised roughly $88 million across multiple rounds, including a Series B in July 2026, and reports 8.9 million monthly active developers along with more than 67,000 community-built integrations and partnerships with major model labs and hardware vendors. The core Ollama runtime is free forever and released as open-source software (MIT license); it wraps llama.cpp's inference engine behind a simple command-line interface and REST API, abstracting away the complexity of model quantization, GPU memory allocation, and model file management. Users can pull and run models such as Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Phi, DeepSeek, Mistral, and gpt-oss from the Ollama model library with a single command, and it supports macOS, Windows, Linux, and Docker. Hardware support spans NVIDIA CUDA, Apple Silicon Metal acceleration, AMD ROCm (Linux only as of 2026), and Vulkan, with automatic CPU fallback via llama.cpp's GGUF kernels when no GPU is present; minimum requirements are roughly 8GB RAM, 10GB free disk, and a 64-bit CPU with AVX2, though 16GB RAM plus a modern GPU is recommended for comfortable use of 7B-14B parameter models. Ollama's key differentiator versus cloud-only LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini) is that inference happens entirely on the user's own machine when running locally, meaning no per-token cost, no data leaving the device, and full offline capability. For workloads that exceed local hardware, Ollama offers a paid Ollama Cloud service with three tiers: Free ($0, 1 concurrent model), Pro ($20/month or $200/year, 3 concurrent models and roughly 50x more cloud usage than Free), and Max ($100/month, 10 concurrent models and roughly 5x more usage than Pro). Ollama states that cloud prompt and response data is never logged or used for training. Ollama's target audience includes developers, AI hobbyists, privacy-conscious enterprises, and researchers who want to experiment with or deploy open-weight LLMs without depending on a third-party API. Its closest alternatives include LM Studio, GPT4All, llama.cpp directly, Jan, and cloud-hosted inference platforms like Groq, Together AI, and Replicate.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ollama is an open-source platform that lets developers and enthusiasts download, manage, and run open-weight large language models directly on their own computers. Founded in 2023 by Jeffrey Morgan and Michael Chiang, both Y Combinator alumni, the company is based in Palo Alto, California, and has raised approximately $88 million through a Series B round completed in July 2026.

The project has grown rapidly, reporting 8.9 million monthly active developers and more than 67,000 community-built integrations, with partnerships across major model labs and hardware vendors. Its core product remains free and open source, with a newer paid cloud tier for users who need more compute than their local hardware provides.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Ollama wraps the llama.cpp inference engine behind a simple command-line interface and REST API, letting users pull and run models like Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Phi, DeepSeek, Mistral, and gpt-oss with a single command, without manually managing quantization or GPU memory allocation.

It runs on macOS, Windows, Linux, and Docker, with GPU acceleration support for NVIDIA CUDA, Apple Silicon Metal, and AMD ROCm (Linux only), plus an automatic CPU fallback path so models can run on machines without a dedicated GPU.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ollama's local runtime is free forever under an open-source license, with unlimited use on a user's own hardware and no usage-based fees for local inference.

For workloads beyond local hardware capacity, Ollama Cloud offers a Free tier ($0, 1 concurrent model), a Pro tier ($20/month or $200/year, 3 concurrent models and roughly 50x the cloud usage of Free), and a Max tier ($100/month, 10 concurrent models and roughly 5x the usage of Pro).

features.title: Local LLM inference
features.description: Runs large language models entirely on a user's own hardware, with no data sent to a third-party API by default.

features.title: Simple CLI and REST API
features.description: Pull, run, and manage models with single commands or integrate Ollama into applications via its REST API.

features.title: Broad model library
features.description: Provides one-command access to popular open-weight models including Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Phi, DeepSeek, Mistral, and gpt-oss.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs natively on macOS, Windows, and Linux, plus an official Docker image for containerized deployments.

features.title: GPU and CPU acceleration
features.description: Supports NVIDIA CUDA, Apple Silicon Metal, and AMD ROCm acceleration, with automatic CPU fallback when no GPU is available.

features.title: Automatic model management
features.description: Handles model downloading, quantization, and memory allocation automatically, abstracting away llama.cpp's complexity.

features.title: Ollama Cloud
features.description: Optional paid cloud tiers offload inference to Ollama's infrastructure for larger models or higher concurrency than local hardware supports.

features.title: Community integrations
features.description: Supports more than 67,000 community-built integrations connecting Ollama to other developer tools and applications.

pros: Core local runtime is completely free and open source with no per-token cost
Runs entirely offline, keeping data on the user's own device for privacy-sensitive use cases
Broad model library with one-command installation of popular open-weight models
Cross-platform support across macOS, Windows, Linux, and Docker
Large, active developer community with tens of thousands of integrations
cons: Local model performance is limited by the user's own hardware, especially without a capable GPU
Cloud tier pricing (Pro at $20/month, Max at $100/month) adds cost for users needing more concurrency or larger models
AMD GPU acceleration is Linux-only as of 2026, limiting Windows AMD users to CPU inference
Managing local models and disk space can become cumbersome as model libraries grow
As a young, fast-growing company (founded 2023), product and pricing details are still evolving

faqs.question: Is Ollama free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ollama's local runtime is free forever and open source under the MIT license, with unlimited use on your own hardware. An optional paid Ollama Cloud tier exists for offloading heavier workloads.

faqs.question: What models can I run with Ollama?
faqs.answer: Ollama supports a wide range of open-weight models including Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Phi, DeepSeek, Mistral, and gpt-oss, downloadable from the Ollama model library with a single command.

faqs.question: Does Ollama require a GPU?
faqs.answer: No, Ollama can run on CPU alone via llama.cpp's GGUF kernels, though a GPU (NVIDIA CUDA, Apple Silicon Metal, or AMD ROCm on Linux) significantly improves performance for larger models.

faqs.question: What is Ollama Cloud?
faqs.answer: Ollama Cloud is a paid service for offloading inference to Ollama's infrastructure when local hardware is insufficient, with Free, Pro ($20/month), and Max ($100/month) tiers offering increasing concurrency and usage.

faqs.question: Who founded Ollama?
faqs.answer: Ollama was founded in 2023 by Jeffrey Morgan and Michael Chiang, both Y Combinator alumni, and is based in Palo Alto, California.

faqs.question: Does Ollama send my data to the cloud?
faqs.answer: When running models locally, no data leaves your device. For Ollama Cloud usage, the company states that prompt and response data is never logged or used for training.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Ollama?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives for running LLMs locally include LM Studio, GPT4All, Jan, and using llama.cpp directly, while cloud-hosted inference alternatives include Groq, Together AI, and Replicate.

use_cases.title: Private, offline AI development
use_cases.description: Developers run models locally to prototype AI features without sending data to third-party APIs.

use_cases.title: Privacy-sensitive enterprise deployment
use_cases.description: Organizations with strict data handling requirements run LLMs on internal infrastructure using Ollama instead of external APIs.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises with compliance or data residency requirements

use_cases.title: AI experimentation and learning
use_cases.description: Hobbyists and students experiment with different open-weight models on personal hardware to learn about LLM behavior.

features: Access to full Ollama model library
No data logging or training on prompts
Roughly 50x more cloud usage than Free tier
Roughly 5x more usage than Pro tier

enrichment.short_description: Ombi is a free, open-source, self-hosted media request and user management tool that lets people request movies, TV shows, and music for Plex, Emby, or Jellyfin servers.
enrichment.seo_title: Ombi Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ombi review 2026: free self-hosted media request tool for Plex, Emby, and Jellyfin. Features, Sonarr/Radarr integration, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ombi is a free, open-source, self-hosted media request, user management, and discovery web application for Plex, Emby, and Jellyfin media server owners. The project began life as PlexRequests.Net, created by developer tidusjar, and was renamed to Ombi around January 2017 as its scope expanded beyond Plex-only requests to also support Emby and later Jellyfin. It is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3) and maintained on GitHub under the Ombi-app organization by a community of open-source contributors rather than a commercial company, so it has no headquarters, employee count, or funding. Ombi's core function is letting end users, typically family members or friends with access to a shared media server, browse a catalog and request movies, TV shows, or music without needing direct access to server management tools. When a request comes in, Ombi checks whether the content already exists on the connected media server, and if not, automatically forwards the request to the appropriate companion tool in the self-hosted media automation ecosystem: Radarr for movies, Sonarr for TV shows, and Lidarr (or legacy tools like CouchPotato and SickRage) for music, which then handle downloading and adding the content. Administrators can configure an approval workflow, automatically approve trusted users, manage user accounts (including native Plex.tv, Emby, and local accounts), and send notifications through various channels to keep requesters updated on request status. Ombi runs cross-platform via Windows, macOS, Linux, and Docker, making it a standard component of self-hosted media automation setups alongside Sonarr, Radarr, Prowlarr, and the media servers themselves. Because it is entirely free and open source, there is no pricing tier or subscription; the closest thing to a paid dimension is the underlying compute and storage costs of the self-hosted server it runs on. Ombi's primary audience is homelab operators and small groups who run private media servers and want a polished, self-service way for other users to request new content without giving them admin access. Its main open-source alternatives are Overseerr and its actively maintained fork Jellyseerr, which have become popular for their more modern interface and, notably, appear to have overtaken Ombi in community mindshare in recent years, alongside older tools like Petio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ombi is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application that lets people request movies, TV shows, and music for a Plex, Emby, or Jellyfin media server. It began as PlexRequests.Net, created by developer tidusjar, and was renamed Ombi around January 2017 as support expanded beyond Plex to Emby and later Jellyfin.

The project is released under the GPLv3 license and maintained by open-source contributors on GitHub rather than a commercial company. It has become a standard part of many self-hosted media automation setups, sitting alongside tools like Sonarr, Radarr, and Lidarr.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users browse a searchable catalog and submit requests for movies, TV shows (whole series, specific seasons, or single episodes), or music. Ombi checks whether the content already exists on the connected media server and, if not, automatically forwards the request to Radarr, Sonarr, or Lidarr to handle acquisition.

Administrators can enable an approval workflow, whitelist trusted users for automatic approval, manage accounts across Plex.tv, Emby, and local logins, and configure notifications across multiple channels so requesters stay informed about the status of their requests.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ombi is completely free and open source under the GPLv3 license. There is no paid tier, subscription, or commercial edition; users self-host it on their own hardware or a home server, typically alongside their Plex, Emby, or Jellyfin installation.
Because there is no company behind Ombi, support is community-driven through GitHub issues, forums, and self-hosting communities rather than a paid support contract.

features.title: Movie, TV, and music requests
features.description: Lets users request entire movies, TV series, specific seasons or episodes, and music albums from a browsable catalog.

features.title: Sonarr, Radarr, and Lidarr integration
features.description: Automatically forwards approved requests to Sonarr, Radarr, or Lidarr (and legacy CouchPotato/SickRage) to handle acquisition.

features.title: Approval workflows
features.description: Administrators can require approval for requests or whitelist trusted users for automatic approval.

features.title: Multi-account support
features.description: Supports Plex.tv, Emby, and local user accounts, with user management for shared media servers.

features.title: Duplicate detection
features.description: Checks whether requested content already exists on the connected media server before forwarding a new request.

features.title: Notifications
features.description: Sends configurable notifications through multiple channels to keep requesters updated on request status.

features.title: Cross-platform deployment
features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Docker, fitting easily into existing self-hosted media server setups.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscription cost
Deep integration with the popular Sonarr, Radarr, and Lidarr media automation stack
Supports Plex, Emby, and Jellyfin, covering the most common self-hosted media servers
Flexible approval workflow lets admins balance automation with control
Long track record dating back to its 2016-2017 origins as PlexRequests.Net
cons: No commercial support; relies on community forums and GitHub issues
Interface and development pace have been seen by some users as less modern than newer alternatives like Overseerr or Jellyseerr
Requires a self-hosted server and some technical setup, including API keys for each connected media automation service
No official cloud-hosted or managed version
Maintained by a smaller open-source community than some newer forks in the space

faqs.question: Is Ombi free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ombi is free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no paid tier or subscription.

faqs.question: What media servers does Ombi support?
faqs.answer: Ombi supports Plex, Emby, and Jellyfin media servers.

faqs.question: How does Ombi work with Sonarr and Radarr?
faqs.answer: When a user requests a movie or TV show, Ombi checks whether it already exists on the media server and, if not, automatically forwards the request to Radarr (movies) or Sonarr (TV shows) to handle downloading and adding the content.

faqs.question: What was Ombi called before?
faqs.answer: Ombi began as PlexRequests.Net, created by developer tidusjar, and was renamed Ombi around January 2017 as it expanded beyond Plex-only support.

faqs.question: Can I control who can request content in Ombi?
faqs.answer: Yes, administrators can require manual approval for requests or whitelist trusted users so their requests are automatically approved.

faqs.question: Does Ombi run in Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ombi is commonly deployed via Docker, alongside Windows, macOS, and Linux installation options.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Ombi?
faqs.answer: The most common alternatives are Overseerr and its actively maintained fork Jellyseerr, along with older tools like Petio.

enrichment.short_description: Omeka Classic is a free, open-source content management system for building single-site digital collections and narrative exhibits, built by George Mason University's Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media.
enrichment.headquarters: Fairfax, Virginia, United States (George Mason University)
enrichment.seo_title: Omeka Classic Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Omeka Classic review 2026: free open-source CMS for digital collections and exhibits from George Mason University. Features, hosting options, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Omeka Classic (omeka.org) is a free, open-source web publishing platform purpose-built for museums, libraries, archives, and scholars to publish digital collections and narrative online exhibits. Development began in 2007 within the Center for History and New Media (renamed the Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media, or RRCHNM, in 2011) at George Mason University, and the software was first released publicly in February 2008. It was created because few affordable options existed at the time for cultural heritage institutions to publish standards-based, metadata-rich collections to the web with a blogging-software-simple administrative interface. Omeka Classic uses the Dublin Core metadata standard by default, supports item-level cataloging with unlimited fields via customizable item types, and offers a plugin and theme ecosystem for extending functionality (exhibit building, mapping, geolocation, PDF embedding, and more). As a self-hosted, single-site CMS, Omeka Classic is entirely free to download and install under an open-source license, though users must supply and maintain their own web server meeting its PHP/MySQL requirements. RRCHNM also operates a separate managed hosting service, Omeka.net, and offers paid managed hosting for Omeka Classic installs, with Omeka.net's annual hosted plans ranging from a free tier up to Plus ($35/year), Silver ($75/year), Gold ($350/year), and Platinum ($1,000/year), which vary by storage, number of sites, and available themes and plugins; these hosted plans are a separate product from the free, self-hosted Omeka Classic software itself. Omeka Classic has a sibling product, Omeka S, also from RRCHNM, which is a newer, multi-site, API-first, Linked Open Data-oriented platform for institutions managing multiple related sites from one installation; Omeka S is a separate downloadable product and listed separately, while this entry covers Omeka Classic specifically. Omeka Classic's target audience is academic libraries, museums, historical societies, and individual scholars building focused, single-site digital collections or exhibits, often for a course, research project, or small institutional archive. Its closest alternatives include Omeka S (for multi-site or API-driven needs), CONTENTdm, CollectiveAccess, and general-purpose CMS platforms like WordPress or Drupal when adapted for digital collections work, though none match Omeka Classic's purpose-built focus on standards-based cultural heritage metadata and exhibit-building.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Omeka Classic is a free, open-source content management system built specifically for publishing digital collections and online exhibits. Development began in 2007 at the Center for History and New Media at George Mason University (renamed the Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media, or RRCHNM, in 2011), with the first public release in February 2008.

The platform was created to give museums, libraries, and archives a free, standards-based way to publish collections online with an administrative interface as simple as blogging software, at a time when few affordable alternatives existed for cultural heritage institutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Omeka Classic catalogs items using the Dublin Core metadata standard by default, with customizable item types and unlimited metadata fields to fit different collection needs. Its Exhibit Builder lets curators arrange items into narrative, multi-page online exhibits without writing code.

A plugin and theme ecosystem extends core functionality with features like geolocation and mapping, PDF and document embedding, and custom site design, while its single-site architecture keeps setup and administration relatively simple compared to multi-site platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Omeka Classic itself is completely free and open source; the software can be downloaded and self-hosted at no cost, provided the user supplies a compatible PHP/MySQL web server.

For institutions that do not want to self-host, RRCHNM offers a separate managed hosting service, Omeka.net, with annual plans including a free tier and paid tiers such as Plus ($35/year), Silver ($75/year), Gold ($350/year), and Platinum ($1,000/year), which scale by storage, number of sites, and available themes and plugins.

features.title: Dublin Core metadata cataloging
features.description: Items are cataloged using the Dublin Core standard by default, with customizable item types and fields for specialized collections.

features.title: Exhibit Builder
features.description: Lets curators assemble collection items into narrative, multi-page online exhibits without coding.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Extends core functionality with community and RRCHNM-built plugins for mapping, geolocation, PDF embedding, and more.

features.title: Theme customization
features.description: Offers switchable themes to customize the public-facing design of a collection or exhibit site.

features.title: Standards-based metadata export
features.description: Supports standards-based metadata that make collections interoperable with library and archive systems.

features.title: Self-hosted single-site architecture
features.description: Runs as a self-hosted PHP/MySQL application focused on a single collection or exhibit site per installation.

pros: Completely free and open source to download and self-host
Purpose-built for cultural heritage metadata standards like Dublin Core
Simple, blogging-software-like administrative interface lowers the barrier for non-technical curators
Exhibit Builder makes it easy to turn a collection into a narrative online exhibit
Long track record and active community since its 2008 release, backed by an academic center
cons: Requires users to supply and maintain their own PHP/MySQL web server for self-hosting
Single-site architecture limits institutions that need to manage many related sites from one installation (Omeka S is better suited for that)
Plugin and theme ecosystem is smaller than general-purpose CMS platforms like WordPress
Managed hosting via Omeka.net requires a separate paid subscription for those who do not want to self-host
Development pace is slower than commercially-backed CMS platforms since it is maintained by an academic center

faqs.question: Is Omeka Classic free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Omeka Classic is free and open-source software that can be downloaded and self-hosted at no cost, though you need your own PHP/MySQL web server.

faqs.question: Who created Omeka?
faqs.answer: Omeka was developed starting in 2007 by the Center for History and New Media (now the Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media) at George Mason University, with its first public release in February 2008.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Omeka Classic and Omeka S?
faqs.answer: Omeka Classic is a single-site content management system for one collection or exhibit, while Omeka S is a separate, newer product from the same team that supports multiple sites, an API-first architecture, and Linked Open Data from a single installation.

faqs.question: What is Omeka.net?
faqs.answer: Omeka.net is a separate, paid managed hosting service from RRCHNM that runs Omeka Classic without requiring users to install or maintain their own server, with annual plans ranging from free to $1,000/year.

faqs.question: What metadata standard does Omeka Classic use?
faqs.answer: Omeka Classic uses the Dublin Core metadata standard by default, with customizable item types for specialized cataloging needs.

faqs.question: Who uses Omeka Classic?
faqs.answer: Omeka Classic is primarily used by museums, libraries, archives, historical societies, and individual scholars to publish digital collections and online exhibits.

faqs.question: Can I build online exhibits with Omeka Classic?
faqs.answer: Yes, Omeka Classic includes an Exhibit Builder that lets curators arrange collection items into narrative, multi-page online exhibits without writing code.

use_cases.title: Digital library and archive collections
use_cases.description: Libraries and archives catalog and publish digitized collections online using Dublin Core metadata standards.

use_cases.title: Museum and historical society exhibits
use_cases.description: Institutions build narrative online exhibits from their collections using the Exhibit Builder.

use_cases.title: Academic research and course projects
use_cases.description: Scholars and students publish focused digital collections for research projects or class assignments.
